ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for coin prata or roti prata with mutton curry. FOODON_03302051 (flatbread food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for coin prata or roti prata with mutton curry in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for coin prata or roti prata with mutton curry. SNOMED CT does not catalogue this specific Singaporean Indian dish.

preparation_method: layered dough folded into coin shapes

serving.description: 1 serving (4–5 coin prata pieces with mutton curry)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of coin prata with mutton consists of approximately 4 to 5 small coin-shaped prata pieces accompanied by a portion of mutton curry, totalling approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (plain, cooked) and Mutton Curry — component-based estimation using HPB Singapore Foodo and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for coin prata with mutton as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for plain roti prata (cooked, per 100 g) with ICMR-NIN data for mutton curry (per 100 g), weighted at approximately 55% prata dough and 45% mutton curry by mass, reflecting a typical coin prata with curry serving composition. Energy and macros are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for analogous flatbread-with-meat-curry dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of roti / paratha-type flatbreads and analogous wheat-flour flatbread with curry — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coin prata with mutton curry. GI is estimated at 57 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain roti / paratha-type wheat flatbread (GI approximately 62, medium) adjusted downward to approximately 57 to account for the fat and protein contribution of the mutton curry component, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates the postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (57 × 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 32.1, rounded to 31.9 after accounting for dietary fibre. This estimate is consistent with peer-reviewed findings on mixed flatbread-with-curry meals (Augustin et al., 2015, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving contributes approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two coin prata pieces and request reduced-salt curry preparation, as the cumulative sodium from the spiced mutton curry and salted dough can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (estimated GI 57) and moderate carbohydrate content (22.5 g per 100 g); a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 56 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 32.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume coin prata with mutton in controlled portions, pairing it with a high-fibre vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response given the medium GI and high glycemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 4.6 g per 100 g (11.5 g per serving) from ghee in the prata dough and coconut milk in the mutton curry, alongside moderate cholesterol at approximately 42 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of coin prata with mutton to occasional servings, as the ghee-enriched dough and coconut-milk-based curry contribute saturated fat that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coin prata with mutton is a popular Singaporean Indian dish consisting of small, coin-shaped pieces of crispy pan-fried flatbread served alongside a richly spiced mutton curry. The prata dough is made from plain wheat flour, egg, ghee, water, and salt, layered and folded repeatedly to create a flaky, multi-layered texture before being cooked on a flat griddle until golden and crisp on the outside with a soft, chewy interior. The mutton curry is slow-cooked with onion, tomato, and a blend of whole and ground spices including coriander, cumin, turmeric, and chilli, often enriched with coconut milk to produce a thick, aromatic gravy. The dish is a staple at Singaporean Indian Muslim (mamak) stalls and Indian restaurants, commonly consumed as a breakfast, brunch, or supper item. Nutritionally, a 250 g serving provides approximately 545 kcal, 23 g protein, 27 g fat, and 56 g carbohydrate. The dish is a source of iron and B vitamins from the mutton, and calcium from the ghee and flour. Sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 775 mg per serving, and saturated fat from ghee and coconut milk warrants attention for cardiovascular health. The medium glycemic index (estimated 57) and high glycemic load per serving make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean establishments and contains gluten, egg, and dairy allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for coin prata with mutton as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of HPB Foodo (plain roti prata) and ICMR-NIN (mutton curry) data, which are both approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat flatbread adjusted for the protein and fat contribution of mutton curry. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in the exact macro and micronutrient profile depending on the specific recipe and curry-to-prata ratio used by individual restaurants.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from HPB Foodo (plain roti prata, cooked) and ICMR-NIN (mutton curry) at a 55:45 prata-to-curry mass ratio. Actual values will vary by restaurant recipe, ghee quantity, and curry richness. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in either the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for these components. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for these components. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for these components. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (57, medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for plain wheat paratha/roti adjusted downward for the fat and protein contribution of mutton curry. 6. The dish is classified under Singaporean Indian cuisine; multilingual names are provided in English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish. 8. The food is halal in most Singaporean Indian Muslim establishments but consumers should verify halal certification at individual restaurants.
